---
title: "Cantor set"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    html-math-method: katex
---

We have explored many interesting topological properties of sets so far, and many of them regard intervals and other such simple sets. The cantor set is no such set.

## Construction

We define the Cantor set by recursively removing the 'middle-third' of the interval $[0,1]$. We can define it by the following

$$
C_0 = [0,1]
$$

And

$$
C_n = \frac{C_{n-1}}{3} \cup \left(\frac{2}{3}+\frac{C_{n-1}}{3}\right)
$$

Then the 

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Definition

Let $C_0 = [0,1]$ and recursively define

$$
C_n = \frac{C_{n-1}}{3} \cup \left(\frac{2}{3}+\frac{C_{n-1}}{3}\right).
$$

Then the [Cantor set]{.definition} is the infinite intersection

$$
C = \bigcap_{n\in N} C_n
$$
 
:::

## Properties

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Theorem

A cantor set is 

:::
